heb.paragraph: "Answer me when I call to you, my righteous (ṣedeq) God" (v.1). David addresses God as righteous — the appeal is not to mercy but to justice. "Give me relief when I am in distress." The verb hirhîb means to make wide, to create space. David is cramped by enemies; he asks God to make room.

refs.note: "In your anger do not sin" — Paul quotes Psalm 4:4 directly.
"I lie down and sleep" — the morning/evening pair.

notes.note: "Answer me when I call." The directness of prayer: no preamble, no formality. David calls; he expects an answer.
"The LORD has set apart his faithful servant for himself." The ground of confidence: divine election. David is set apart.
"Tremble and do not sin; when you are on your beds, search your hearts and be silent." Anger is permitted; sin is not. The bed is the place of self-examination.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "how long will you love delusions?" — David addresses his enemies directly. The psalm alternates between God and opponents. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: "be silent" (dōmmū) is the hardest command in the psalm. Calvin: silence before God is the posture of submission. The tongue that would argue must learn to be still. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "tremble" (rigzū) can mean tremble with anger or with fear. Paul’s use in Eph 4:26 interprets it as anger. Either way: strong emotion must not become sin. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "tremble and do not sin" is the OT’s most direct counsel on anger management. Paul takes it verbatim into Ephesians 4:26. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: Psalm 4 completes the day cycle begun in Psalm 3. Morning trust (Ps 3:5) is matched by evening peace (Ps 4:8). Faith covers both. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "search your hearts on your beds and be silent" — nighttime honesty. The quiet of evening strips away daytime defences. In silence, the heart speaks truth. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: An evening companion to the morning psalm (Ps 3). David confronts those who turn his glory to shame (v.2) and reminds them: the LORD has set apart the faithful (v.3). "Tremble and do not sin; search your hearts and be silent" (v.4). The counsel is to self-examination and stillness — a rare call to quiet in the Psalter.

sections.header: Verses 6–8 — “In Peace I Will Lie Down and Sleep”

heb.paragraph: "In peace (shālōm) I will lie down and sleep" (v.8). The Psalter’s most famous evening prayer. Shālōm is not just absence of conflict but complete well-being. David sleeps in total peace because God alone makes him dwell in safety. The trust is exclusive: "you alone, LORD."

refs.note: "The LORD make his face shine on you and give you peace" — the Aaronic blessing that David echoes.
"The peace of God, which transcends all understanding, will guard your hearts" — Paul’s NT development.

notes.note: "Let the light of your face shine on us." The Aaronic blessing personalised. God’s face = God’s favour. One moment of divine favour outweighs a year of harvest.
"In peace I will lie down and sleep, for you alone, LORD, make me dwell in safety." The psalm’s summit. Peace, sleep, safety — all from one source: "you alone."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the simplicity of "lie down and sleep" echoes 3:5. The two psalms form an evening/morning pair, both finding rest through trust. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: "you alone" excludes every other ground of security. Calvin: the Christian who can say "you alone" to God has everything; the one who needs additions has nothing. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "when their grain and new wine abound" — the comparison is with the best economic news imaginable. God’s favour surpasses even prosperity.
The NET: "dwell in safety" (lābetaḥ) means to dwell securely, without anxiety. The safety is not the absence of danger but the presence of God.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "you alone" is the psalm’s theological core. Not God plus security measures. Not God plus good circumstances. God alone is sufficient for sleep. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: this verse has been the church’s evening prayer for millennia. Compline, evening offices, and bedtime prayers draw from this well: peace, rest, divine protection. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the joy from God’s presence exceeds harvest joy. In an agricultural society, a good harvest was the highest temporal blessing. God’s face beats it. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: Many ask "who can show us any good?" (v.6). David answers: God’s face shining on them brings more joy than abundant harvest (v.7). The psalm closes with the perfect sleep: "in peace I will lie down and sleep, for you alone, LORD, make me dwell in safety" (v.8). The emphasis is "you alone" — security comes from one source.

Give me relief from my distress; have mercy on me and hear my prayer.ESVAnswer me when I call, O God of my righteousness! You have given me relief when I was in distress. Be gracious to me and hear my prayer!

rec.text: ""Answer me when I call." The directness of prayer: no preamble, no formality. David calls; he expects an answer."

rows.label: Tremble and Be Still — 4:1–5
rows.text: "In your anger do not sin. Search your hearts and be silent."

hebtext.note: "Answer me when I call to you, my righteous (ṣedeq) God" (v.1). David addresses God as righteous — the appeal is not to mercy but to justice. "Give me relief when I am in distress." The verb hirhîb means to make wide, to create space. David is cramped by enemies; he asks God to make room.

"In peace (shālōm) I will lie down and sleep" (v.8). The Psalter’s most famous evening prayer. Shālōm is not just absence of conflict but complete well-being. David sleeps in total peace because God alone makes him dwell in safety. The trust is exclusive: "you alone, LORD."

thread.text: "In your anger do not sin" — Paul quotes Psalm 4:4 directly.
"I lie down and sleep" — the morning/evening pair.
"The LORD make his face shine on you and give you peace" — the Aaronic blessing that David echoes.
"The peace of God, which transcends all understanding, will guard your hearts" — Paul’s NT development.

themes.note: Faith peaks: the evening prayer of total trust. Worship: the Aaronic blessing echoed. "You alone" is the psalm’s theology.
